data.title: Invesco Main Street Mid Cap Fund Q4 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Investment objective

The fund seeks capital appreciation.

Fund facts

Fund AUM ($M)

2,484.26

Portfolio managers

Adam Weiner, Matthew Ziehl, Belinda Cavazos, Joy Budzinski, Magnus Krantz, Raman Vardharaj

Manager perspective and outlook

  • US equities advanced in the fourth quarter despite renewed volatility amid economic crosscurrents and seemingly mixed investor sentiment toward artificial intelligence (AI) valuations.
  • The US government shutdown appeared to weigh on confidence as key economic data releases were delayed. Once released, the initial third quarter GDP estimate showed robust 4.3% growth.
  • Labor market conditions softened, as November’s jobs report showed weaker non-farm payrolls and unemployment rose to 4.6%, a four-year high. Consumer spending remained resilient but showed strain among lower income households.
  • Inflation moderated but November’s 2.7% headline CPI remained above the Fed’s 2% target.
  • The Fed cut the federal funds rate twice during the quarter, signaling a cautious shift toward easing while emphasizing its commitment to balance inflation pressures with growing labor market risks.
  • Despite the mid-quarter volatility spike, strong corporate earnings and Fed rate cuts fueled equity gains. The Russell Midcap Index returned 0.16% while the S&P 500 Index returned 2.66%.
  • Regardless of market sentiment and near-term economic trends, our investment process favors better-managed companies with strong balance sheets and competitive positioning. We seek to outperform through stock selection while minimizing any top-down macro, factor and sector exposures relative to the index.

Portfolio positioning

We maintain our valuation discipline and our focus on companies with competitive advantages and skilled management teams that we believe are executing better than their peers. These companies historically tend to have higher profit margins and returns on invested capital, rising market shares and consistently strong pricing power. As of quarter end, all sector weights were within +/- 3% of the Russell Midcap Index.

The largest additions to the fund during the quarter included the following:

Take-Two Interactive Software (TTWO) is a leading video game developer and publisher that we believe offers an attractive risk/reward profile, supported by its strong franchises and growth potential in interactive entertainment.

Tractor Supply (TSCO) is a leading retailer of rural and lifestyle products. We initiated a position after recent share weakness created a compelling entry point, in our view.

Carlyle Group (CG) is a leading alternative investment manager that has delivered strong results under CEO Harvey Schwartz; we believe the stock remains undervalued relative to industry peers.

Datadog (DDOG) is a leading provider of cloud monitoring and security solutions that we believe is positioned for above-average growth as enterprises migrate workloads to the cloud.

Yum! Brands (YUM) has outperformed peers in same-store sales at Taco Bell and KFC; we see additional upside as management has prioritized its strongest brands while reviewing strategic options for its lagging Pizza Hut segment.

Positions sold during the quarter included the following:

Tyler Technologies (TYL) was sold because the software provider’s revenue growth has decelerated, which reduced our conviction in its upside potential.

Blue Owl Capital (OWL), an alternatives investment manager, was sold in favor of businesses that have more diversified exposure beyond private credit.

Ascendis Pharma (ASND) was sold because increasing regulatory and competitive headwinds reduced our conviction in the biotech company’s growth prospects.

Tapestry (TPR), which owns luxury brands like Coach and Kate Spade, was sold after its share price rallied in 2025, bringing it close to our upside target.

CACI International (CACI) was sold as its strong performance made its forward risk/reward profile less compelling to us.

HealthEquity (HQY), which manages health savings accounts (HSAs), was sold because a lower interest rate environment may put pressure on the significant interest revenue it historically earns from HSA cash balances.

Performance highlights

The fund’s Class A shares at net asset value (NAV) returned 0.60% for the quarter, outperforming the Russell Midcap Index, which returned 0.16%. The fund’s outperformance mainly resulted from stock selection in the health care, industrials and real estate sectors. Weaker stock selection in the consumer discretionary, IT and consumer staples sectors partially offset these results.

Contributors to performance

MongoDB posted robust results, with total revenue exceeding expectations and growth in cloud revenue accelerating. We maintain a constructive view of the company following its recent stock rally, supported by our confidence in the newly appointed CEO’s ability to scale the business and deliver profitable growth.

ATI outperformed as earnings exceeded expectations and management raised full-year earnings-per-share (EPS) guidance above consensus. Strong demand for jet engines – a key earnings driver – provides visibility into 2027, while the stock has been trading at a discount to larger peer Howmet (not a fund holding), a gap we believe should narrow over time.

Guardant Health reported strong quarterly results, driven by sales momentum across its diagnostics solutions and aided by its strategic partnerships with leading testing centers. Management reported that its core business segments turned cash flow positive earlier than expected and signaled to us continued strength by raising its forward revenue guidance.

Detractors from performance

BellRing Brands, which sells Premier Protein products, has faced rising competitive headwinds. Increased distribution of Coca-Cola’s Fairlife brand and the entrance of other market players have pressured BellRing’s market share, creating in our view potential challenges for the company’s future growth trajectory.

Bullish is a globally focused institutional cryptocurrency exchange. While we maintain a constructive view of the company’s fundamentals, its share price underperformance seems to have been driven by broader investor sentiment tied to recent weakness in cryptocurrencies.

Royal Caribbean underperformed as cruise stocks retreated from summer highs. Its stock lagged following management guidance that indicated negative impacts from one-time costs and expected tax headwinds. We believe the company remains better positioned than peers, supported by solid customer demand and a best-in-class balance sheet that should allow it to deliver new ships and take market share from competitors.

About Risk

Derivatives may be more volatile and less liquid than traditional investments and are subject to market, interest rate, credit, leverage, counterparty, and management risks. An investment in a derivative could lose more than the cash amount invested.

* Alpha (cash adjusted) is a measure of performance on a risk-adjusted basis. Beta (cash adjusted) is a measure of relative risk and the slope of regression. Sharpe Ratio is a risk-adjusted measure calculated using standard deviation and excess return to determine reward per unit of risk. A higher Sharpe ratio indicates better risk-adjusted performance. Information Ratio is a measurement of portfolio returns beyond the returns of a benchmark, usually an index, compared to the volatility of those returns. Standard deviation measures a fund's range of total returns and identifies the spread of a fund's short-term fluctuations. Tracking Error is defined as the expected standard deviation of a portfolio's excess return over the benchmark index return. The up and down capture measures how well a manager was able to replicate or improve on periods of positive benchmark returns and how severely the manager was affected by periods of negative benchmark returns. Maximum Drawdown is the maximum observed loss from a high to a low of a portfolio, before a new high is attained. Maximum drawdown is an indicator of downside risk over a specified time period. Weighted Average Market Cap is a measure of the average size of company held in a portfolio. The percentage of the portfolio invested each company, or its weight, is multiplied by its size (market capitalization). An average of the weighted size of all companies held is then calculated. Price/earnings measures the price per share relative to the earnings per share of the company while excluding extraordinary items. Price to book measures the firm's capitalization (market price) to book value. Est. 3-5 year EPS (Earning per share) growth measures the earning per share growth from FY3 to FY5. ROE is the Return on Equity that measures the fund's annual return relative to total shareholders' equity. This ratio evaluates how quickly investments can be turned into profits. Long-term debt to capital measures a fund’s financial leverage by calculating the proportion of long-term debt used to finance its assets relative to the amount of equity used for the same purpose. A higher ratio indicates higher leverage. Operating margin measures the profit a fund makes for every dollar of sales after paying the variable expenses. Contribution to Return measures the performance impact from portfolio holdings over a defined time period. It takes into account both weight and performance of the portfolio holdings. Contribution to Return is calculated at security level.

data.title: Student Living EduVation Financials Sag As It Launches U.S. IPO
data.description:
Student Living EduVation’s Growth Rate And Margins Are Dropping

Student Living EduVation (Holdings) Corporation (SDLV) has filed proposed terms for a $19 million U.S. IPO, according to an amended F-1/A registration statement.

The company provides rental housing options to students in the greater Hong Kong area.

Its topline revenue growth rate has dropped sharply, despite a small base, and its major margin metrics are also falling quickly.

The firm is thinly capitalized, faces intense competition, and is subject to a wide variety of market threats, from fragmentation to government regulatory policy risks.

The SDLV IPO appears grossly overvalued, so my outlook on it is to Avoid it.

What Does Student Living Do?

The company provides housing solutions to domestic and international students studying at various educational institutions in Hong Kong.

It generates most of its revenue from sub-leasing properties it leases, as the revenue contribution pie chart shows here:

Education Advisory services (producing referral fees to SDLV) are provided to students via a related party partner, Meta Academia, so the company has material revenues from a related party and is subject to concentration risk in this regard.

The firm also has a limited number of suppliers, producing concentration risks in the process.

SDLV leases residential properties near universities in Hong Kong, converts them into student dormitories and provides ongoing cleaning, maintenance and security services.

The company Chairman and CEO of the company is Mr. Chun Kwok Ng, who has been with the firm since 2022 and has a financial background in the education consulting industry and the accounting industry.

What is Student Living’s Market?

The company's market is currently limited to Hong Kong and is exhibiting limited supply, forcing students to expend considerable time, effort and money to secure their own lodgings.

The market is seeing a growing number of students coming in from mainland China and other regions, and Student Living provides an integrated set of services to help these students.

The government’s initiatives to enhance the city’s reputation for drawing students have led to increased investment and a positive overall regulatory environment in Hong Kong, according to a 2024 market report by Cushman & Wakefield.

However, a shortage of student beds was expected to worsen in the coming years due to insufficient hostel capacity, with one forecast showing a supply shortfall of 47,000 in recent years.

SDLV faces competition from agencies, individual room landlords, hostels, educational referral providers and other alternative accommodation providers.

Recent industry trends include a greater degree of professionalization of the industry as investors look at student housing as a more predictable and stable real estate asset class, integrated service offerings and catering to mainland China students.

However, the industry faces challenges since it relies heavily on student visa policies by the government, college admission quotas and other education policies that impact the supply and demand of students for education in Hong Kong.

SDLV’s strengths include its integrated business model, property locations near colleges and comprehensive support services and social/cultural activities to improve student retention.

Its weak points are that it relies almost exclusively on leased properties for dormitory supply, exposing the company to lease renewal and rent increase risks, and its operations are limited to Hong Kong, resulting in geographic concentration.

Opportunities on tap for SDLV include expanding its dorm portfolio, both within the greater Hong Kong region and in Malaysia, and continuing and developing partnerships for more housing supply and educational advisory providers.

The company is exposed to the potential of student declines due to government or college policy changes; it is also subject to changing macroeconomic conditions and intense competition from alternative lodging providers of many types.

Student Living’s Recent Financial Results

The company's recent financial results are shown in the table below.

From a small base, revenue has grown quickly but at a decelerating rate of growth, while gross margin, operating margin and net margin have all fallen sharply in the most recent half-year reporting period.

Cash flow from operations has increased recently.

Student Living’s IPO Plan

SDLV has filed to sell 3.75 million shares of common stock at a propose midpoint price of $5.00 per share, for gross proceeds of $18.75 million, excluding customary underwriter over allotments.

The lock-up period for the company, shareholders and employees will be for 180 days after the closing of the IPO, which typically occurs several days after initial open market trading.

Management is seeking an enterprise value of $105 million, a chart float ratio of 15.8% and net tangible book value dilution to new shareholders of $3.41.

SDLV plans to use the net proceeds from the IPO as follows:

We currently intend to use the net proceeds from this Offering for [i] the Group’s Hong Kong business development; [ii] building the Group’s presence in the Southeast Asian market; [iii] developing our student residence and dormitory management system; and [v] for working capital and general corporate purposes.

(Source - SEC.)

The firm’s roadshow is not available for online viewing.

Management said the company is not a party to any legal actions that would have a material adverse effect on its financial condition or operations.

The only listed underwriter of the IPO is D. Boral Capital.

Student Living’s Growth Rate And Margins Are Falling

Student Living seeks to obtain capital U.S. public capital market investors, despite a rapidly slowing revenue growth rate and margin compression across major metrics.

Many Asian companies have sought to raise the new Nasdaq minimum IPO amount of $15 million, but so far in 2026, there have been no successful Asian company IPOs.

I’m not surprised by this, as their generally poor performance in previous years has effectively "burned" their investor base in the U.S., so now that the Nasdaq has rightfully increased the minimum amount, they have been unable to close transactions.

The firm’s growth strategy includes expanding its dormitory network through developer collaborations, providing short-term rental options and entering new markets like Malaysia.

SDLV has already planned a 1,000-student dormitory project in Malaysia.

The market for student housing in Hong Kong is large and faces a significant ongoing shortage of beds for the foreseeable future, so Student Living has positive industry growth dynamics in its favor.

The company is a "foreign private issuer" and "emerging growth company," which means management can provide substantially less information to shareholders.

Many of these types of companies, especially Asian firms, have performed poorly or even been delisted from U.S. stock exchanges after their IPO.

Also, trade relations between China and the U.S. have been volatile in recent years, so the company's ability to continue to be a listed U.S. stock is subject to potentially rapid regulatory changes from either China or the U.S.

As to valuation, management is asking public IPO investors to pay an EV/Revenue multiple of 34x and an EV/EBITDA multiple of 57x, both of which appear excessive.

Given the highly speculative nature of the company, its thin capitalization, sharply falling growth rate and margin compression, and numerous risks to investors, my outlook on the SDLV IPO is to Sell (avoid it).

Expected IPO Pricing Date: To be announced.

data.title: Tourmaline Oil: Deep Value Remains As Major Inflection Point Approaches
data.description:
Introduction

The last time I covered Tourmaline Oil (TRMLF) (TOU:CA), I stated that “There's Deep Value In Canada's Top Natural Gas Producer,” as Tourmaline continued to prove their resilience, disciplined capital allocation and significant strategic expansion, allowing them to capture the industry’s long-term potential.

With a solid report released recently and the company advancing on their long-term plans, Tourmaline remains very attractive, standing to benefit from significant macro tailwinds that can benefit the company, its country and the commodities they produce.

Internal Developments

Tourmaline reported a solid 2025 overall, continuing their best-in-class capital efficiencies and growing their natural gas production significantly alongside the rest of the business, with relatively stable cash flow from operations and a significant ramp-up in CAPEX for their projects, all while holding ~75 years' worth of drilling inventory.

As mentioned, the company expects the following couple of years to continue delivering relatively weak free cash flow, as most of the cash from operations would go into developing their projects, expecting a significant jump from 2028 thanks to the NEBC Montney gas/condensate complex and the premium international sales with strong margins.

They also announced the sale of their Peace River High asset to Canadian Natural Resources (CNQ) for net proceeds of C$765 million, helping them reduce net debt to about C$1.5 billion, as they say during their Q4 Earnings Call. I like both of these companies and I’ll reiterate that Tourmaline arguably has some of the best management in oil and gas on the planet, and I’m sure they wouldn’t just sell these for no reason.

Especially since they’re exposed so much to AECO prices, reducing their net debt by such a big chunk is not bad in this environment, improving their resilience and potentially cleaning the balance sheet while making room for future purchases.

Financially, based on Tourmaline’s latest report, we continue to see a solid position, with an overall manageable amount of leverage, especially after the asset sale mentioned above, which is not visible in this report since that was completed in February.

Again, given the drop in FCF, I really wouldn’t expect some massive dividends other than the base - which remains sustainable on top of the maintenance CAPEX with NYMEX/AECO at $1.50/Mcf (assuming no growth CAPEX), while the rest until ~$3.50/Mcf goes into the significant growth projects they’re working on, and the “excess” goes into additional returns and investments, although their excess FCF would grow significantly from 2028 onwards.

Sure, the current base dividend is not sustainable at current prices, but the company’s expected growth should more than cover it in the future, and the recent asset sales should help them stabilize and buy enough time until the growth projects are completed.

Macro Developments

Since we’re talking about commodities that depend significantly on international markets, the ongoing Iran conflict will be essential for Tourmaline as well, especially since we’re talking about a region that plays a key role not only for oil, but also natural gas, with the initial strikes against Qatar for example causing a ~50% jump in one day in European gas on the Monday following the attack.

Again, this remains a matter of time and will ultimately depend on the goal here, while I’ll reiterate that Iran is a massive (armed) natural fortress, and that factor combined with several other logistic, natural and especially religious aspects would make a regime change nearly impossible and certainly way too costly from any point of view (especially timewise) - with American troops on the ground being quite mandatory.

Not only would the risk of failure be significant, but it can also trigger a wider conflict in the region, putting even more pressure on these commodities - and more - which is why I expect (and hope, from a humanitarian point of view) that this is just a “quick skirmish,” which so far seems to be the case given Trump’s recent statements where he said that this may end “pretty quickly.”

Still, until this is over and while the Strait of Hormuz stays blocked (which Iran stated will be the case for as long as the attacks persist), we’re very likely to see these effects ripple and scale throughout the global economy - even if oil were to drop to ~$84 at the moment of writing this as G7 states are working on a coordinated release of oil from reserves.

These commodities are ultimately vital for the global economy from much more than just the direct uses of oil and gas, with >90% of today's sulfur (which is key for sulfuric acid manufacturing) for example being recovered from oil and gas, which is key for fertilizers, metal processing and extraction (for several commodities including copper, nickel, zinc, etc.), which by itself can have significant effects on the broader economy, and that’s just one “simple” branch of a massive global supply chain. And that’s on top of shipping companies avoiding even the Suez Canal due to the critical level of risks, potentially leading to significant supply chain disruptions, inflation, and more.

Sure, the longer these high prices and “war premiums” last for, the better it can be for Tourmaline and the industry in the near-term, but looking at it from a medium- and long-term perspective, it can actually be worse for an already weak global economy following the post-pandemic rate hikes, while the fundamentals of oil and gas were already fragile, with production from many countries being at record highs (especially if high prices persist) despite weak demand (which can be even worse due to high prices).

I remain very bullish on Canadian gas, and given the country’s cheap and abundant commodities, weather and potential legislative support, the state and Tourmaline stand to benefit significantly from a potential boom in data center demand. This way, not only would the company not need to search extensively for international premiums, but local commodity prices can help lift these commodities significantly, with AECO prices recently going through a significant slump, which was at 4-decade lows not long ago.

Again, Alberta is already working in this direction, with a dedicated “AI Data Centre Strategy” to potentially attract $100 billion in AI data center infrastructure investments (about a year ago), while the federal government is also reportedly working on something in this direction.

Alongside the country’s “Grand Bargain” MOU regarding the potential pipeline and emission system change, these can help domestic producers reach international markets for better pricing while attracting foreign capital and potentially boosting the local ones as well, which is a step forward for the region.

Especially following Trump’s tariffs and risk for more political pressure, Canada is rightfully looking for new allies, and there’s certainly the possibility of seeing its ties with Asia and Europe deepen over the following years, with a new trade deal with China seen recently, for example.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, the company will always depend on the commodities they produce, which means they will continue to be influenced by politics, wars, weather, etc., with volatility being the only guaranteed thing we have, despite their strong long-term potential.

Meanwhile, potential delays or weaker prices can put pressure on Tourmaline's valuation, as the market will definitely tax them if the major inflection point is delayed even by a bit, as that comes with costs that can affect their balance sheet given their base dividend coverage.

Valuation

For Tourmaline's DCF to Equity, I'll start from their guidance, which I'll reduce quite a bit on purpose to account for a stronger margin of safety, without accounting for net borrowings since I want to highlight their pure potential and they don't need to rely on debt going forward. Note that everything is converted into USD.

That said, let’s assume a FCF of $500 million in 2026, $450 million in 2027, followed by a jump to $1.5 billion in 2028, $1.8 billion in 2029, $1.95 billion in 2030 and $2.2 billion in 2031, followed by a 4% CAGR until year 10 given the country’s and commodity’s long-term potential, followed by a 2.5% terminal growth rate, and a conservative discount rate of 11% to take into account the potential volatility, political pressure and uncertainty this comes with by default, with 388.7 million shares outstanding implied by the per share calculations from the Q4 report.

This way, we get to an equity value of $20.37 billion, for an intrinsic value of $52.41 per TRMLF share, or about C$71.13 for TOU:CA, which is above the current $46.18 and C$62.77, respectively, despite using conservative assumptions.

Conclusions

Tourmaline Oil remains a Strong Buy, backed by significant organic potential despite near-term pressure, standing to benefit from their ongoing developments and long-term macro tailwinds.

There are certainly risks, and volatility will definitely persist, but the long-term potential of the company, the commodity and even the country can offset them significantly, potentially playing a very important role in our future.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Capital One's Acquisition Of Discover Won't Save It From Declining ROE & ROA
data.description:
Investing Thesis

Capital One Financial Corporation’s (COF) return on equity (“ROE”) and return on assets (“ROA”) have been materially and steadily declining every year since FY2021, when they were 21.45% and 2.80%, respectively. They were respectively at 8.14% and 0.92% by the end of FY2024. Capital One then made a material acquisition of Discover Financial Services in May 2025. Once integrated into Capital One’s financial reporting, there was a drop in FY2025 ROE to 2.66% and in ROA to 0.38%. Even factoring in the potential to return over time to Capital One’s 5-year average ROE of 11.53% (ROA 5-year average is 1.44%), the Company’s excess returns in ROE above its cost of equity (“COE”) are short-lived as they will eventually decline to the COE, which I estimated will happen within 10 years in an Excess Return Model (the model is a form of discounted cash flow analysis generally used to value financial firms). Looking at the model’s output, I estimate the value per share at $160.90, 11.6% lower than COF’s current market price of $182.02 at the time of writing this article. Given the major, global economic shocks in the commodity and financial markets related to the new war on Iran that will soon be impacting the consumers that make up most of the clients of Capital One, and the ongoing chaos of trade wars uprooting global supply chains, the outlook for COF is even more bearish. I rate the stock as a Strong Sell.

Company Overview

Founded in 1988 and headquartered in McLean, Virginia, Capital One Financial Corporation (the “Company”) is the holding company of Capital One, NA, a bank primarily focusing on consumer and commercial banking. The Company’s customer deposits fund its lending, with its loan to deposit ratio (“LDR”) ranging from 90% to 96% over the past five years (at the end of FY2025, after the merger with Discover, LDR was 96%). According to its profile on Seeking Alpha, the Company operates over three segments: Credit Cards, Consumer Banking, and Commercial Banking. Deposits consist of savings accounts, checking accounts, money market deposits, negotiable order of withdrawals, time deposits, and sweep accounts. It offers credit card loans, personal loans, auto loans, retail banking loans, commercial and multifamily real estate loans, and commercial and industrial loans.

The largest event impacting the Company over the last five years was its $35.3 billion acquisition of Discover Financial Services, finalized on May 18, 2025. It was an all-stock deal for 1.0192 shares of COF for each Discover share. While the Company may very well benefit in the long run from this acquisition, for the near-term it appears that the Company will have a tough climb to return to its five-year historical average ROE of 11.53%.

Review of Book Value of Common Equity

As of December 31, 2025, Capital One’s book value of common shareholders’ equity has grown from the end of FY2020 at a compound annual growth rate (“CAGR”) of 14.35%. This sounds impressive, but it is artificially inflated by the Discover Financial integration. If you look at the CAGR through 12/31/2024 (i.e., right before the acquisition of Discover in Q2 2025), it is only 0.26%. Making matters worse, the peak book value of common shareholders’ equity from FY2021 through FY2024 came at the end of FY2021, when it was $56.18 billion. It then dropped precipitously to $47.74 billion in FY2022 (largely due to the COVID-19 pandemic’s disastrous impact on consumer finances). Book value of common equity then increased annually to $55.94 billion at the end of FY2024. Taking into account the acquisition of Discover, the book value of common equity at the end of FY2025 was $108.21 billion.

As of December 31, 2025, COF’s book value per share is $173.11, and its tangible book value (book value less goodwill and intangible assets) per share is $100.98, both below COF’s current market price of $182.02. The Company’s stock has fallen from $241.44 on December 31, 2025, but it is still priced higher than its book value per share. This is historically consistent since FY2021, with the only two times book value was higher than market value being in FY2022 and FY2023. The following table displays the last five years’ worth of market prices, book prices, and tangible book prices per share.

Ratio Analysis

I took a look at historical ratios (calculated using Capital One’s 10-Ks and 10-Qs) to give me an indication of how Capital One has performed. A series of ratios on profitability, financial strength, liquidity, and efficiency, all using figures as reported, are shown in the following table.

Net Interest Margin measures the difference between interest income and interest expenses, with a higher margin being better. Capital One has grown its net interest margin annually from 5.66% in FY2021 to 7.40% in FY2025.

ROE shows how much net income is generated by the average common shareholder’s equity. Please note, net income is what remains after income attributable to the non-controlling interests (such as preferred stock) is paid out. This figure has declined annually, with FY2021 having an ROE of 21.45% and FY2025 having an ROE of 2.66%. This is significantly below COF’s COE of 9.14%, found using the Capital Asset Pricing Model (“CAPM”). See details on the CAPM for COF later in this report.

ROA measures net income against average total assets of Capital One. This measure also declined annually from 2.80% in FY2021 to 0.38% in FY2025.

The provision for credit losses ratio shows the amount of provisions for credit losses as a percentage of net loans. This number increased annually from 4.21% in FY2021 to 5.43% in FY2025. The provision for credit losses measure also reduces net income and increases the allowance for credit losses, a contra-asset account on the balance sheet.

Several ratios for financial strength were reviewed, with higher percentages showing greater financial strength. Tier 1 leverage ratio shows a bank’s core capital to its total assets. Common Equity Tier 1 (“CET1”) risk-based capital ratio shows a bank’s common equity (i.e., exclusive of preferred stock and qualifying minority interests) against its risk-weighted assets. Tier 1 risk-based capital ratio shows a bank's equity capital and disclosed reserves against its total risk-weighted assets. Finally, total risk-based capital ratio shows the sum of Tier 1 and Tier 2 capital divided by the bank’s risk-weighted assets. All these measures for Capital One are significantly above regulatory minimums for all years reviewed.

The Company’s LDR is a percentage of how much of the Company’s deposits are utilized to fund loans. Having a ratio below 100% means all of the bank’s lending is funded internally, whereas a ratio higher than 100% means the bank is dependent on outside financing to finance its growth. Capital One’s LDR ranged from a low of 90% in FY2024 to a high of 96% in FY2025. This leaves some room for Capital One to increase its lending backed by deposits.

The efficiency ratio shows non-interest expenses per dollar of revenue, with a lower ratio being better. Looking at FY2021 through FY2024, this ratio ranged from a low of 54.44% in FY2021 to a high of 55.95% in FY2022. In FY2025, it increased to 57.08%.

Revenue growth less non-interest expense growth, when positive, shows that revenue is growing faster than expenses, as opposed to a negative ratio which shows the company is accumulating expenses faster than it is growing revenues. This figure ranged from a low of negative 13.87% in FY2021 to a high of 1.39% in FY2023, ending at negative 5.33% in FY2025.

In summary, these ratios show that while Capital One fares favorably in its financial strength and liquidity ratios, it has some issues with its profitability as well as its efficiency, particularly after its acquisition of Discover Financial in FY2025.

Excess Return Model

An Excess Return Model calculates the value of a firm by summing equity capital currently invested and the present value of excess returns that the firm is expected to make in the future. This model is ideal for financial services companies with complex and uncertain cash flows. To accurately calculate the present value of future returns, several assumptions and inputs need to be decided upon.

I reviewed the historical net incomes attributable to the common stockholders. From $11.97 billion in FY2021, net income attributable to the common shareholders declined annually to a low of $2.18 billion in FY2025. Given FY2025 includes just over two quarters of income from the acquisition of Discover Financial, I used FY2025’s net income of $2.18 billion as the base net income for the model, and used the average of the book values of common equity of $108.21 billion in FY2025 and of $55.94 billion in FY2024 as the base level book value of common equity used to calculate an ROE of 2.66%. For earnings per share, I used FY2025’s net income divided by the weighted average common shares outstanding on a diluted basis (541 million shares) to arrive at $4.03 earnings per share and annual dividends per share of $3.20 were used in the model.

To calculate the discount rate, or COE, I used the CAPM. I used COF’s 5-year monthly beta of 1.14 (provided by Seeking Alpha), an equity risk premium of 4.38% as of March 1, 2026 (as provided by Professor Aswath Damodaran, a professor at Stern School of Business at New York University), and a risk-free rate of 4.23% (the 10-year Treasury note). Plugging these numbers in comes to a COE of approximately 9.22%.

Given Capital One’s net income attributable to common shareholders was at a five year low, and there are expectations of the Discover Financial acquisition improving Capital One’s performance, I estimated a high growth period of five years, during which time the Company’s ROE gradually increases from its current low of 2.66% in year one to 11.50% in year five (just shy of its five-year average ROE of 11.53%). I used the starting book value of common equity of $108.21 billion as of December 31, 2025 for year one of the model. I also used a retention rate of 20.60% for the first five years of the model, which was estimated by taking 1 minus the current dividends per share divided by the current earnings per share.

After the 5-year high-growth period, I gradually adjusted the ROE downwards over the next five years to 9.75% in the Terminal Year. I obtained this ROE from Professor Aswath Damodaran’s research showing an average ROE of 9.75% for Regional Banks (the closest comparable industry available) as of January 2026. I also adjusted the retention ratio gradually upwards from 20.60% to 30.77% in the Terminal Year. Finally, over the final five years of the model I gradually adjusted the COE upwards from 9.14% to 9.75% in the Terminal Year driven by increasing the beta from 1.14 to 1.20 at the period of stable growth of the Terminal Year, as well as nudging it slightly higher to meet the Company’s ROE of 9.75% (showing no excess return in the perpetuity of the Terminal Value).

The first three years of the model have negative excess returns, reflecting the Company’s low ROE taking several years to build higher to exceed the annual COE. The next six years have positive excess returns, reflecting a higher ROE than COE. In the final year, and for the Terminal Value, the model shows the COE meeting the ROE, resulting in a Terminal Value of $0.

The present value of these returns, as discounted using the cumulated COE (which considers the gradual growth in COE in years six through ten), is negative in years one through three, before turning positive throughout the rest of the projected periods. These sum to equal a present value of equity excess returns of negative $7.63 billion, as shown in the following tables.

When added to the $108.21 billion of equity invested at December 31, 2025, and then divided by the numbers of shares outstanding (much higher than historical levels due to the share-based consideration paid to holders of Discover Financial), results in an estimated value per share of $160.90, or $21.12 lower than today’s share price of $182.02. This implies a downside of 11.6% from current levels.

Peers

Betas

One caveat to using the 5-year monthly beta of COF in the CAPM to find COE is that much of that 5-year time does not have Discover Financial incorporated within the Company, so there may be some distortion in the latter period of regressed beta. However, looking at the 2-year monthly beta (with Discover Financial being included since Q2 2025, so nearly half the time) shows it at 1.39, much higher than the 5-year monthly betta of 1.14 that I used to calculate COE. A higher beta will demand a higher COE, so my use of the 5-year beta gives some relief to COF in the Excess Return Model.

I also reviewed the 2-year and 5-year monthly betas of Capital One’s peers, as shown in Seeking Alpha, to see whether their betas would present an industry beta that would lower Capital One’s COE. The mean and median 2-year monthly betas were 1.31 and 1.24, respectively. The mean and median 5-year monthly betas were 1.21 and 1.21, respectively. All of these figures are higher than the beta of 1.14 that I selected. This means if there is an error in my selection of beta, it is more likely to the upside than the downside. A higher beta would raise the COE, and in turn, would make the excess returns of ROE that much smaller in the Excess Return Model. See the following table for a look at COF’s peers and their respective betas.

Short Interest

COF has had a significant drop from its 52-week high of $259.64 on January 6, 2026 to $182.02, its current price. Year-to-date COF is down 24.9%. To see if this was artificially adjusted downward by a high number of short sellers, I looked at COF’s short interest as well as the short interest of its peers. The mean and median short interest of COF’s peers was 1.92% and 1.41%, respectively. COF’s short interest of 1.30% was below both of these figures and close to the minimum of the group (1.04)%. These are all fairly low short interests and thus there is a lot of room to short COF without needing to worry about a short squeeze. See the following table for more details on short interest of COF and its peers.

ROE & ROA

Finally, I reviewed the ROEs and ROAs of COF’s peers to see how they compared to COF. The group’s ROEs ranged from a low of 10.22% (far above COF’s current ROE of 2.66%) to a high of 33.99%. The mean and median ROEs were 18.66% and 15.61%, respectively. Both of these figures are much higher than COF’s 5-year average ROE of 11.53%. See the following table for more details.

In terms of ROAs, the group ranged from a low of 1.36% to a high of 4.83%, with a mean and median of 2.96% and 3.44%, respectively. All these figures are higher than COF’s ROA of 0.38%.

These low performance measures indicate a lower equity value should be ascribed to COF compared to its peers. Again, see the following table for more details.

Risks & Other Factors

The key risk to having a Strong Sell rating is that the stock might revisit its recent heights, either in a short-term relief rally after the Iran war concludes, or long-term as Discover Financial’s acquisition is digested. According to Capital One, the acquisition created a global payments platform with over 70 million merchant acceptance points in more than 200 countries and territories. The Company cites $2.7 billion in pre-tax synergies and, by 2027, it will be >15% accretive to adjusted non-GAAP earnings per share. Their 2027 return on invested capital is projected to be 16%, with an internal rate of return of >20%. These projections are potential positives for the stock price, however, the Company’s past shows multiple warning signs that these projections might not come about. Additionally, the Company’s concentration in consumer lending typically carries a risk of loss that is higher than commercial lending and can be adversely affected by an economic downturn. The likelihood of an economic downturn increases substantially when trade wars heat up, as they are currently, or if the independence of the Federal Reserve comes into question. The war in Iran has also roiled commodity and financial markets and it is unclear how long this factor will negatively influence the overall economy.

Conclusion

Given all of the above, I believe the potential downside of COF far outweighs its factors pointing to a higher stock price. The Company’s acquisition of Discover Financial may in the end be a positive that will bring the stock to new heights, but my estimation is that the turnaround is very iffy. My estimated value per share of $160.90 using an Excess Return Model reflects that potential downside of 11.6%. I rate COF as a Strong Sell.

data.title: MongoDB: May Not Be A Hypergrowth Company Like Before
data.description:
Introduction

MongoDB, Inc. (MDB) recently reported decent Q4 results, but investors didn’t like the guidance, as it showed that MDB may not be a hypergrowth company any longer, which sent its share price down 25% and caused many analysts to lower their price targets significantly. I wanted to go over the report in more detail and give some comments on the outlook and what I think could happen to its share price in 2026.

By the Numbers

Revenue for the quarter came in at around $695m, up 27% y/y, and beating estimates by almost $26m. Not a bad performance here. The Atlas cloud engine saw 29% growth and accounted for over 72% of total revenue. It is good that the biggest contributor is growing at quite a respectable pace. Overall subscription revenues grew 27%, with enterprise advanced growing around 20%, which surprised a lot of investors and analysts, as the growth was the best in the last 2 years.

Of course, it is obvious why. The management said that AI-driven companies helped propel the company’s revenues to new levels, which led to some pretty big contracts. To make it a full analysis, the professional services, which are 3% of total revenues, also grew around 26% y/y, bringing in $22m.

Let’s go over the company’s profitability and efficiency. Q4 non-GAAP EPS came in at $1.65, which beat estimates by 18 cents. GAAP and non-GAAP gross margins were stable at 73% and 75%, respectively. These haven’t changed at all from last year, which is not a bad sign at all. Despite the high costs of paying for Amazon’s (AMZN) AWS and Microsoft’s (MSFT) Azure to host their Atlas cloud service, MDB managed to maintain very healthy gross margins across the board.

GAAP operating margins were actually positive this time around, but just positive, at 0.1% vs -3.4% last year. That is an improvement of 330bps. In terms of adjusted operating margins, the company saw record-high margins of 23% vs 21% last year, beating their own guidance. The fantastic revenue growth, as well as disciplined spending in operating expenses, helped here. The company is able to scale a lot more efficiently without increasing expenses too much, as seen by its maintained gross margins.

Let’s take a look at the company’s financial situation. MDB finished the year with a massive improvement in its cash position. Its cash and equivalents improved by around $600m, while its short-term investments declined by around $543m. The total liquidity comes in at around $2.38B, which is already quite good, but on top of that, the company has no debt on its books. So, all the resources the company generates can go back into the growth of the company, which will help it maintain its leadership and grab even more market share.

Speaking of resources, cash flow from operations exploded to over $500m vs $150m last year, with free cash flow coming in at $500m. The company barely spent anything on capex as it is an asset-light company that doesn’t own its servers but rather pays AWS and Azure to use theirs. The improvement in cash flow is substantial.

On top of these, what I would call rather stellar results overall, the company gave us a look at the next quarter’s expectations, which seemed to overshadow the past results. Revenues for Q1 ’27 are in the range of $659m-$664, $661.5m at the midpoint, and in line with analysts’ estimates, with full-year 2027 revenues to be in the range of $2.86B to $2.9B, or $2.88B at the midpoint, missing estimates of around $2.9B. Analysts were expecting the growth rate to remain in the mid 20s, ranging from 22%-24%, but now the guidance implies around 17% growth, which is a significant slowdown. So even though the numbers were close to what analysts expected, these results were just not enough, and may as well have been misses, as the company’s high premium means it is priced to perfection, and a slowdown in revenue is not a good look.

On the day of the results, MDB’s share price plummeted over 25%, which I think is a bit of an overreaction, but that is what happens when the company’s valuation is already stretched. A double beat was not good enough to counteract the slowdown in future revenue performance. Following the report, many analysts slashed their price targets significantly. Barclays cut to $370 from $440, while Needham slashed it to $300 from $500, but still maintained a buy rating.

Comments on the Outlook

The slowdown in MDB’s cloud growth is not what was promised to investors when its share price was bid up over $440 a share back in December of 2025. Everyone expected to see this hypergrowth sustained much longer, but now that there are some troubles brewing, the market repriced the stock almost immediately. We can see a clear shift in how analysts and investors now look at MDB. They don’t see it as a hyper-growth disruptor but rather a moderate growth enterprise player. It is not a bad transition, in all fairness, but that means that the company’s share price needs to come back to earth since those days seem to be over, unless the company somehow manages to come right back.

MDB products provide a lot of value to their customers in general. MDB wouldn’t see such decent growth all around if the products were useless. Over 40% of MDB’s largest customers, which have over $100k in ARR, now use two or more platform features, like Stream Processing, Search, or Vector. Let’s take a look at Atlas Vector Search. The management said that this segment doubled in 2025 due to its criticality for AI models. LLMs require vector data to retrieve specific and relevant facts through a process called RAG, or Retrieval-Augmented Generation.

MongoDB is looking to shift from a database company to an AI platform company, and now, with the new CEO stepping in, he wants to make the company a “generational data platform of choice” for the new AI era. The recent SaaSpocalypse that wreaked havoc on many SaaS businesses’ valuations, including MDB, continues to be a threat, but MDB’s underlying business remained solid and has continued to see decent growth, so I don’t think it is going to go away anytime soon.

I would like to see how the company’s Atlas cloud performs over the next few quarters, and whether the adoption increases going forward, as that seems to be the main growth driver for 2026. No one would like to see the growth slowing down here over the next year, as that would cause its share price to correct further.

Should you buy after the drop?

Overall, I believe that there will be a lot of growth in revenues and other metrics because the company is following the money, and AI and data center infrastructure are where all the money is. Hyperscalers continue to spend ridiculous amounts on data center and AI infrastructure, so there is no shortage of cash out there, and if MDB can capitalize on that, we will see its share price go back up.

I also think that the company is still a bit too pricey for me to invest. Even after the massive plunge in share price, it is still trading at over a 46 forward PE ratio. It is a lot more attractive than before, but if its growth continues to slow, investors are not going to pay such premiums going forward, and I expect to see further multiple contraction. I’d like to see how the growth story develops over the next couple of quarters; therefore, I am giving MongoDB a Hold rating for now.

data.title: Commodities Lead Major Asset Classes By Wide Margin This Year
data.description:
The war in Iran has roiled the outlook for financial markets and the global economy, but commodities are clearly benefiting from the turmoil as prices rise for energy and other raw materials.

The shift in leadership for the major asset classes has turned sharply in favor of a broad measure of commodities, based on a set of ETFs through yesterday’s close (Mar. 11). The WisdomTree Enhanced Commodity Strategy Fund (GCC) is up 15.3% year to date, far ahead of the rest of the field.

Commodities are the exception. Energy is the main beneficiary of the fighting, which has disrupted oil exports from the Gulf region. The interruption of energy flows has spilled over into other areas that are affecting supply chains in some agricultural sectors, for example.

Various metals are also in the crosshairs. “The Gulf is a major supplier of aluminum, and disruptions could tighten supply chains for advanced manufacturing,” said Tony Pelli, practice director of supply chain security and resilience at BSI Consulting, a global risk management firm. “Aluminum prices are already rising, and further disruption could increase input costs for automotive, aerospace, and construction manufacturing in the US and Europe.”

Soaring energy prices will likely fall once the war ends, but as of this writing, the odds still appear low that the conflict will cease in the immediate future. Meanwhile, energy markets continue to price in a worst-case scenario. Oil prices rose in early trading on Thursday despite a coordinated announcement the day before from world leaders to release 400 million barrels of oil from their strategic reserves.

Economists are struggling to estimate the breadth and depth of the war’s effects on the global economy, but you don’t need sophisticated models to recognize that the negative consequences will continue until the hostilities end.

“If this disruption goes on longer, we will see faster drawdowns [of existing oil supplies],” said Amin Nasser, head of state-owned oil giant Saudi Aramco. “There would be catastrophic consequences for the world’s oil market, and even more drastic consequences for the global economy.”

There’s never a good time for war, but the current crisis comes at a time of pre-existing macro challenges for the US economy. Economists at Wells Fargo sum up the situation in a research note published on Wednesday:

Independent of the oil price spike, employment growth has effectively stalled over the past year, with payroll levels treading water amid weak hiring outside a handful of industries (Figure 2). At the same time, real personal income excluding transfer payments—a metric closely watched by the National Bureau of Economic Research recession dating committee—has lost momentum as wage growth has slowed and inflation has remained sticky. A renewed rise in energy prices would likely push inflation back above 3% in the near term, mechanically eroding real income and placing additional strain on households, particularly the lower- and middle-income households that tend to allocate a greater share of spending to fuel costs.

The good news is that the repair and recovery will likely start as soon as the conflict winds down. Unfortunately, that point doesn’t appear imminent.

“If the hostilities wrap up in relatively short order, we see little reason for investors to expect a lasting market impact,” predict strategists at AllianceBernstein. “That’s largely because the economic impact wouldn’t be lasting either. But geopolitical conflicts are complex and unpredictable. If things drag out, the situation—and our assessment of the impact—could change. Time will tell.”

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: GBP/USD At The Crossroads: Will Cable Break The 1.3437 Resistance?
data.description:
By Zain Vawda

GBP/USD has found support in early trade around the 1.3360 handle. A stellar rally from the Monday lows ran into resistance provided by the 100-day MA at 1.3437 as risk-off sentiment returned and the US dollar strengthened.

Will USD strength keep further gains at bay?

The rise in GBP/USD today comes as the US Dollar Index retreats from a multi-month resistance level at 99.57.

If this level holds and DXY continues to decline, then GBP/USD could retest the 100-day MA and finally the psychological 1.3500 handle.

Cable's fate is very much tied to the US dollar at the moment, while the US dollar's is tied to overall risk sentiment as well as inflationary concerns. Given the steep rise in oil prices and the impact it may have on gasoline prices, markets expect a potential 3% rise in headline inflation next month.

These developments are also keeping the US dollar supported, as rate cut expectations have fallen from around 66 bps two weeks ago to around 30 bps as of this morning.

US Dollar Index Daily Chart, March 12, 2026

What next for the dollar and GBP/USD?

Financial markets remain primarily focused on the duration of current geopolitical conflicts and the resulting supply shocks.

Recent emergency measures intended to mitigate oil supply disruptions may have inadvertently signaled to investors that global leaders anticipate a prolonged period of tension rather than a swift de-escalation, a sentiment reflected in yesterday's volatile oil and equity performance.

Furthermore, market sensitivity to optimistic military updates from the Trump administration appears to be waning, as investors grow increasingly skeptical of claims regarding the achievement of strategic objectives.

While the FX market continues to be driven by immediate headlines, the broader signals emanating from equity and energy sectors currently suggest a bullish outlook for the US dollar, especially if the conflict drags on for a prolonged period.

Tomorrow's US PCE data may also take a backseat to the geopolitical situation, as markets know that any inflation numbers at the moment are out of touch with the new reality.

Technical Analysis - GBP/USD

From a technical perspective, the pair is currently caught in a tug-of-war between a long-term downtrend and a significant horizontal support zone.

While the British pound has shown resilience, the US dollar remains dominant due to safe-haven flows stemming from the ongoing conflict in the Middle East and concerns over the Strait of Hormuz.

Technical Indicators

  • Simple Moving Averages (SMA):
    • 100-period SMA (Blue): Currently at 1.34376. This acts as immediate dynamic resistance. The price is currently trading below this line, confirming a bearish bias on the 4-hour timeframe.
    • 200-period SMA (Dark Blue): Sitting higher at 1.35543. The fact that the 100-SMA is below the 200-SMA indicates that the medium-term trend remains firmly to the downside.

The RSI period-14 is currently at 51.20, which is usually a sign that momentum has shifted to potentially favor bulls.

Trading Outlook

Bearish Case: If the pair fails to break above the 100-period SMA (1.34376), expect a retest of the 1.33788 support. A break below the recent low of 1.33332 would open the door to 1.3250.

Bullish Case: A sustained move above 1.3450 would invalidate the immediate bearish structure, potentially leading to a test of the 200-period SMA near 1.3550.

GBP/USD Daily Chart, March 12, 2026

data.title: URNM: ETF With Global Exposure In The Uranium Industry
data.description:
Fast Facts About the Sprott Uranium Miners ETF

The Sprott Uranium Miners ETF (URNM) was launched on December 3, 2019 and tracks the North Shore Global Uranium Mining Index. URNM has 26 holdings, a trailing 12-month yield of 2.54% and an expense ratio of 0.75%. Distributions are paid annually. It is a large and liquid ETF, with $2.4 billion of AUM (assets under management) and an average daily dollar volume of $69 million. The fund’s issuer Sprott is an asset management company headquartered in Toronto, Canada, with a focus on precious metals and critical materials investment strategies. Sprott also has merchant banking and resource lending services for natural resources companies.

Underlying Index

As described in the prospectus by Sprott, the underlying index targets exchange-listed companies that have a significant portion of their operations related to uranium. The index was revised in December 2025. Its main current rules are listed below. To be included in the index, companies must have at least:

  • $125 million in market capitalization (and are excluded if they fall below $100 million).
  • $100,000 in 3-month average daily traded value (and are excluded if they fall below $75,000).

The index selects at least 25 constituents, relaxing some constraints if necessary. It is split in two parts:

  • 82.5% of asset value in miners, explorers, developers and producers.
  • 17.5% in companies holding physical uranium, uranium royalties, or other non-mining assets.

Companies are weighted separately in each part based on free-float market capitalization, with maximum limits of 20% in any constituent and 50% in aggregate in those weighting more than 5%. The index is reconstituted semi-annually and rebalanced quarterly. I will use as a benchmark a metals and mining index excluding gold and silver, represented by the iShares MSCI Global Metals & Mining Producers ETF (PICK).

URNM Portfolio

Based on company domicile, Canada is the heaviest country in the portfolio (60.5%), followed by Australia (18.3%), the U.S. (7.3%), Hong Kong (5.2%), Kazakhstan (4.8%) and the U.K. (3.9%).

The portfolio is very concentrated. Indeed, the top 10 holdings, listed in the next table, represent 80.8% of asset value and the heaviest position weighs 21.45%. A large share of the physical uranium part is allocated to a Canada-listed closed-end fund of the same issuer: Sprott Physical Uranium Trust (U.U:CA).
